coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots: Efficient fluorescent chemosensor for the selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Manjot
Mehta, Surinder K.
Kansal, Sushil Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have been fabricated using lemon juice by a simple and cost effective thermal pyrolysis technique, without the consumption of any harmful chemicals or solvents. N-GQDs displayed excellent water solubility, good stability and excitation dependent down conversion fluorescence (FL). The obtained N-GQDs displayed a strong FL emission at wavelength around 440nm at optimum excitation wavelength of 340nm. FL spectrum of N-GQDs showed highly selective and sensitive quenching with the addition of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP). The calibration curve from Stern − Volmer equation showed a linear range from 0 − 4μM with obtained detection limit of 420nM. This work demonstrated simple, green and facile synthesis of N-GQDs and the obtained N-GQDs were successfully applied for the detection of mutagenic water pollutant, TNP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GQDs
Thermal pyrolysis
Fluorescent sensor
TNP

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of ionic strength and pH on stability of N-GQDs
FL detection of TNP by N-GQDs
Influence of metal ions and phenolic derivatives on FL intensity of N-GQDs
Mechanism of FL quenching of N-GQDs with TNP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Typical UV–vis absorption (b) FTIR (c) XRD and (d) AFM image and the corresponding height profile of N-GQDs.
Typical HRTEM images of the as synthesized N-GQDs.
Typical high resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1s. (b) O 1s (c) N1s and (d) EDX spectrum of N-GQDs.
FL emission spectra of N-GQDs at different excitation wavelength (a) from 280 to 330nm. (b) from 340 to 430nm. (c) Effect of NaCl concentration and (d) pH on the FL intensity of N-GQDs.

(a) FL spectrum of N-GQDs (0.5mg/mL, 1mL) and N-GQDs+ TNP (100μM, 1mL) (b) Effect of various metal ions and substituted phenols on the FL response of N-GQDs (c) FL spectrum of N-GQDs in the presence of different nitroaromatic substances (d) Bar graph representing the effect of nitroaromatic analogues upon the FL intensity of N-GQDs.

(a) FL behaviour of N-GQDs in the presence of various TNP concentrations (from top to bottom (0–50μM)) (b) FL emission curve of N-GQDs with increased concentration of TNP (Inset: Stern Volmer plot for the quenching process).
The Stern-Volmer quenching constant and correlation coefficients of TNP.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots: Efficient fluorescent chemosensor for the selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dr. S.S.B. University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. S.S.B. University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have been fabricated using lemon juice by a simple and cost effective thermal pyrolysis technique, without the consumption of any harmful chemicals or solvents. N-GQDs displayed excellent water solubility, good stability and excitation dependent down conversion fluorescence (FL). The obtained N-GQDs displayed a strong FL emission at wavelength around 440nm at optimum excitation wavelength of 340nm. FL spectrum of N-GQDs showed highly selective and sensitive quenching with the addition of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP). The calibration curve from Stern − Volmer equation showed a linear range from 0 − 4μM with obtained detection limit of 420nM. This work demonstrated simple, green and facile synthesis of N-GQDs and the obtained N-GQDs were successfully applied for the detection of mutagenic water pollutant, TNP.

ce:section.ce:para: Explosives based on nitroaromatic and nitroamine substances have adverse effects on human mankind, environment and also associated with global security issues as these are widely used as warfare agents by militaries. Specific and sensitive detection of these explosives have attained much attraction in recent years [1,2]. Photosensitive nanomaterials such as semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) have gained tremendous attention owing to their fascinating applications in sensing and molecular imaging. But due to toxic and biologically incompatible nature of SQDs, their use as probes for in vivo diagnosis is restricted. Thereby, the exploration of safe, environment friendly and less toxic alternatives has begun [3].

Graphene, a sp2 hybridized two dimensional carbon material possesses remarkable characteristics like extensive surface area, superior electrical conductivity, fascinating mechanical flexibility and excellent chemical inertness [4]. Optical luminescence is not observed in pristine graphene due to the absence of band gap, so optical properties and band gap of graphene can be modified by converting its size to the nanolevel and named as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [5]. GQDs have recently appeared as marvellous nano dimensional materials as the better replacement for SQDs [3]. GQDs have sparked great interests as they possess extraordinary properties such as low cytotoxicity, high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, easy surface modification and outstanding biocompatibility. Stable photoluminescence is one of the most attractive characteristic features of GQDs and can be easily amended by numerous approaches such as modification in size and shape by doping with various heteroatoms and functionalization with polymeric molecules [6]. GQDs can be used as an excellent probes for fluorescence sensing [7], bioimaging [8], photocatalysis [9], photoelectrochemical and electrochemiluminescence sensing [10,11]. These can be fabricated by using top down and bottom up synthetic approaches. Top down methodology involves the exfoliation and decomposition of various carbonaceous materials using different processes such as acidic oxidation, electrochemical exfoliation and laser ablation [12–15]. However, these strategies require multistep reactions, drastic processes and harsh conditions, still yield uncontrolled size and morphology of nanoparticles. Bottom up method includes carbonization and dehydration of carbon based precursors via thermal pyrolysis, microwave and hydrothermal technique [16–18]. Different groups have also synthezized GQDs from natural and easily available carbonaceous precursors [19–21].

Persistent organic pollutants (POPs) acquire characteristic features such as high stability, less degradability, toxicity and long retention period in environment. Substituted phenols and its derivatives, important members of POPs are extensively used in many industries like food, cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, pesticides and photography. These can cause severe health problems, for instance, birth disorders, hormonal imbalances, drowsiness, headaches and cyanosis [22,23]. These compounds are considered as environmental pollutants owing to their high toxicity and recalcitrance to degradation and thus, the successful elimination of these perilous contaminants is a matter of concern [24].The trace level analysis of these compounds involves various traditional methods which are expensive, complicated and time consuming such as gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS), chemiluminesence (CL), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). Therefore, relatively simple, effective and low cost methods for the determination of POPs are required [25].

Herein, we have used a simple, green and a single step approach for the synthesis of N-GQDs by using thermal pyrolysis technique. The as synthesized N-GQDs were further characterized using numerous spectroscopic and analytical techniques such as FTIR, XRD, AFM, HRTEM, XPS, EDX and others. Highly fluorescent and stable N-GQDs were employed for the efficient selective and sensitive detection of carcinogenic and toxic organic compound, i.e. TNP in aqueous media.

Lemon juice was procured from local market of Chandigarh. Sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium acetate tetrahydrate [(CH3COO)2 Mg·4H2O], mercury acetate (HgOAc)2, zinc sulphate heptahydrate (ZnSO4·7H2O), ferric chloride (FeCl3), 4-nitrophenol (NP), 2,6-dichlorophenol (DCP), 2,4,6-trichlorophenol (TCP), 2-nitrotoluene (NT), nitrobenzene (NB), p-nitrobenzaldehyde (PNBA) and p-nitrobenzoic acid (PNB) were obtained from Merck (India). 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) were provided by SD Fine Chemicals and Fisher Scientific, India respectively. All the chemicals were used as received without prior modification and stock solutions were prepared using distilled water.

Water soluble N-GQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing lemon juice. 40mL of lemon juice was taken in a petri dish and then it was heated at 200°C for 20min. During gradual heating, the color of lemon juice changed to dark brown, indicating the formation of N-GQDs. Then, the obtained sample was further dispersed in water and the solution was centrifuged at 5000rpm for 10min. The resulting suspension of N-GQDs was stored at 4 °C for further use.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) spectrum of N-GQDs was recorded on Systronics-2202 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was taken on Nicolet iS50 (Thermoscientific) FTIR spectrometer equipped with a diamond ATR in the range of 500–4000cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was analyzed by PANalytical X’Pert PRO diffractometer equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54056Å) in the 2θ range of 10–80° in order to investigate the structure of N-GQDs. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were taken on SPM Multimode 8 (Bruker). Morphology of the synthesized N-GQDs was observed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), using FEI Technai F20 microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected using AMICUS, Kratos (Shimadzu) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Energy dispersive X ray (EDX) pattern was determined by X Flash 6130 transmission electron microscope (Bruker). Hydrodynamic diameter was obtained by using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Horiba SZ-100). Fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded on a F-7000 FL (HITACHI) spectrophotometer using quartz cuvette of 1cm path length.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ionic strength and pH on stability of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Stability of N-GQDs was examined in the presence of high ionic strength conditions. For this, aqueous solutions of NaCl with concentrations varied from 100 to 500mM were prepared and added to N-GQDs solution (0.5mg/mL, 2mL) and the corresponding FL spectra were recorded. For examining the influence of pH on N-GQDs stability, pH of the N-GQDs solution was changed from 2 to 11 with the help of 0.1M HCl and 0.1M NaOH and the respective FL spectra were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL detection of TNP by N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the fluorescent detection of TNP, aqueous solution of N-GQDs (0.5mg/mL, 1mL) was mixed with solution of different concentrations of TNP in a quartz cuvette. The solutions were mixed properly at room temperature. An excitation wavelength of 340nm was used for recording the FL emission spectrum of N-GQDs. An interference effect from different metal ions, substituted phenols and nitroaromatic compounds was also investigated. Briefly, an aqueous solution of respective compounds (100μM, 1mL) were introduced into the N-GQDs (0.5mg/mL, 1mL) solution and the corresponding changes in the FL emission spectrum were recorded.
In this work, a facile, green and effective thermal pyrolysis procedure was used for the fabrication of highly luminescent and water soluble N-GQDs.

Fig. 1 a showed the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-GQDs. It exhibited two absorption peaks, one peak at 236nm and a shoulder band at 345nm. An absorption peak at 236nm was assigned to the π→π* transition of aromatic sp2 region [26] and a band centered at 345nm was ascribed to the n→π* transition of CO group [9,27]. Functional groups were determined by FTIR technique and Fig. 1b illustrates the FTIR spectral analysis of the synthesized N-GQDs. The absorption peaks at 781 and 889cm−1 correspond to the rocking vibrations of −CH2 plane [28]. Vibration peaks at 1174cm−1 and 1395cm−1 could be assigned to COH/COC and CN/CN groups, respectively [7,9,29]. Peaks centered at 1699 and 2935cm−1 confirmed the existence of CO and CH groups, respectively [9,28]. A broad peak at 3435cm−1 was ascribed to the presence of OH and NH bonds in N-GQDs [9,30]. Fig. 1c exhibited the XRD pattern of the synthesized N-GQDs and a broad peak was obtained at 2θ =220, which might be attributed to (002) plane of graphitic structure of the prepared N-GQDs [28,31]. Fig. 1d displayed AFM image of N-GQDs. The topographic height of N-GQDs was found to be in the range of 1.4 −2.7nm.

HRTEM images (Fig. 2 ) revealed that the as synthesized N-GQDs were having almost spherical morphology with lattice spacing of 0.21 and 0.24nm, corresponding to the hexagonal lattice planes (1100 and 1120) of graphene, respectively. [12,32,33].

N-GQDs showed intense FL and maximum FL intensity was obtained around 440nm when the sample was illuminated at an excitation wavelength of 340nm. Fig. 4 a exhibited that FL intensity of N-GQDs was increased when excitation wavelength was varied from 280 to 330nm but emission wavelength remained unchanged. Fig. 4b elucidated that further increment in excitation wavelength from 340 to 430nm; there was decrease in FL intensity with the bathochromic shift in emission wavelength. The observed excitation dependent pattern could be ascribed to the presence of surface defects, free zigzag sites and different emissive traps present in N-GQDs [41].

Ionic strength and pH experiments were carried out to examine the stability of the fabricated N-GQDs. Insignificant change in FL intensity was observed in Fig. 4c with increasing NaCl concentrations, depicting the high stability of N-GQDs under high ionic strength environments. Effect of pH on the stability of N-GQDs was demonstrated in Fig. 4d. FL intensity of N-GQDs executed negligible change in the wide pH range of 3–10, proving its potential use in various FL based applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of metal ions and phenolic derivatives on FL intensity of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is an important parameter in order to assess the performance of the proposed chemosensor. To explore the selectivity of N-GQDs, FL measurements were performed in the presence of various interfering species such as metal ions, substituted phenols and nitro aromatic substances. Selectivity of the sensor was evaluated by plotting fluorescence intensity ratios of N-GQDs against various interfering species. As seen in Fig. 5 a, FL spectrum of N-GQDs was greatly quenched with the TNP addition. Fig. 5b showed the effect of various metal ions and substituted phenols on the FL emission intensity of N-GQDs. Significant quenching was observed in the case of TNP, whereas no noticeable change in FL intensity was found in the presence of other interfering species. Effect of different nitroaromatics such as NT, NB, PNBA, NP, DNP and PNB on the FL emission intensity of N-GQDs was also determined. As shown in Fig. 5(c,d), TNP showed a remarkable quenching effect on the FL emission intensity, whereas other nitroaromatics quenched the emission spectrum to the smaller extent. Thus, the proposed sensor was very useful for differentiating TNP from other nitroaromatic substances.

Due to high specificity of N-GQDs for the sensing of TNP, the sensitivity of as developed sensor was further investigated. Fig. 6 (a,b) shows the variations in FL intensity of N-GQDs in the presence of increased TNP concentrations. Quenching response was described with the help of Stern-Volmer equation:
F0/F=1+Ksv[C]
Where F0 and F corresponds to the FL intensity at 340nm in the absence and presence of quencher (TNP), respectively, C is the quencher concentration and Ksv is the Stern Volmer constant which represents the affinity between quencher and fluorophores [3].

The calibration plot was made by plotting the FL intensity ratios (F0/F) against TNP concentration and graph was displayed in inset of Fig. 6b. It was clear from the figure, FL of N-GQDs was linearly quenched with the TNP addition in the concentration range from 0 to 4μM. The obtained regression equation was shown below:
y=1.04076+0.14915 x
where y and x represents the FL intensity ratios and TNP concentration, respectively with correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9934. Detection limit of 420nM was calculated by using the formula 3σ/m, where σ represents the standard deviation and m was the slope of the calibration curve. So, the linear working range and lowest limit of detection was found to be 0–4μM and 420nM, respectively. The Ksv constant was estimated to be 1.4×105 M−1 and this large value of quenching constant indicated the selectivity of the sensor for the determination of TNP. Table 2 represents the FL quenching constant and correlation values of TNP. The results clearly illustrated the high selectivity and sensitivity of the proposed fluorescent chemosensor towards the detection of harmful organic pollutant, i.e. TNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of FL quenching of N-GQDs with TNP
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs demonstrated intense fluorescence, good stability, outstanding water solubility and п network even without surface passivation/modification and further used as a probe for the detection of nitroaromatic phenolic explosive compound, i.e. TNP. N-GQDs displayed strong fluorescence in aqueous solution which is significantly quenched with the addition of TNP. The fluorescence quenching process can be described in three possible ways: (i) fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (ii) molecular interactions and (iii) formation of Meisenheimer complex [42]. Spectral overlap was observed between emission spectrum of donor (N-GQDs) and absorption spectrum of acceptor (TNP), resulting in quenching of N-GQDs via FRET mechanism operated between N-GQDs and TNP (Fig. S1). The amino groups and pyridinic nitrogen of N-GQDs bind with hydroxyl groups of TNP via electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding, leading to the significant quenching. Also, when high concentrations of TNP were added into the aqueous solution of N-GQDs, red shift in emission peak was occurred, verifying the presence of molecular interactions between TNP and N-GQDs. Among TNP, DNP and NP, TNP being highly acidic, quenched FL strongly as compared to DNP and NP, indicating preferential selectivity of the sensor towards the detection of TNP [42,43]. Hydrodynamic mean diameter of N-GQDs acquired from DLS was shifted from 5.6nm to 37.7nm in the presence of 100μM of TNP and attributed to the formation of non-fluorescent Meisenheimer complex (Fig. S2) [44].

In this work, we demonstrated simple and efficient thermal pyrolysis technique for the fabrication of highly fluorescent N-GQDs. The characterization techniques revealed the successful introduction of nitrogen atom into the graphitic framework of N-GQDs. The as prepared N-GQDs provided a sensing platform for the highly efficient determination of TNP based on FL quenching mechanism of N-GQDs in aqueous medium. The FL behaviour of N-GQDs was highly selective for TNP, i.e. the FL intensity of N-GQDs did not change significantly in the presence of different metal ions and nitroaromatic compounds. The Stern − Volmer plot gave a linear correlation between FL intensity of the assay and concentration of analyte over the range 0–4μM with detection limit of 420nM. Hereby, the present work paves a facile and green approach for the fabrication of N-GQDs for highly selective and sensitive detection of TNP.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support received under UGC-BSR scheme (grant No. F. 25-1/2014-15(BSR)/No. F. 5-91/2007/(BSR)) and TEQIP-II project grant of Dr. S.S. Bhatnagar UICET, Panjab University, Chandigarh.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.02.026.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Manjot Kaur received her MSc in chemistry from Thapar University, Patiala in 2012. She is currently a Ph.D student in Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh since 2014. Her main research interest is focused on the study of synthesis and photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots, as well as their applications in the field of photocatalysis and fluorescent sensing.

Dr. Surinder Kumar Mehta, Senior Professor Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh and Director SAIF/CIL/UCIM is highly active in the significant areas of research like metallosurfactant chemistry, nano electrochemical sensors, synthesis and application of semiconducting nanoparticles and colloidal chemistry that includes novel formations for drug delivery applications. He is also Coordinator, Chandigarh Region Innovation Knowledge Cluster (CRIKC), Local coordinator MHRD initiative GIAN, Coordinator UGC CAS and Chief Editor Panjab University Research Journal (Science). Dr. Mehta has more than 236 publications in international journals of repute with h-index of 30, i10 99 and is an author of about 10 books/chapters. He has been nominated as member of several DST, CSIR and UGC national committees. He is receipt of renowned DAAD and JSPS fellowships several times, Bronze medal from Chemical Research Society of India (CRSI), authors award by Royal Society of Chemistry (UK), Haryana Vigyan Ratna award and Prof. W.U. Malik Memorial Award of Indian Council of Chemists (ICC) for the year 2015 for his outstanding contribution in research. He has been a visiting scientist to several countries including UK, Germany, Japan, USA and France. Dr. Mehta has guided 10 post-doctoral, 30 Ph.D. and 40 Master students. He has completed/completing 5 DST; 4 CSIR; 6 UGC, 2 Indo-German and one Indo-Japan Research/Exchange projects. He was lead Coordinator from India for one training school on Waste water Treatment at University of Hull, UK sponsored by DST/British Council under Newton Bhabha Fund (2016). Recently he has been awarded a Fellowship of the Royal Society of Chemistry (FRSC) by the Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC).

Dr. Sushil Kumar Kansal is an academician and researcher of international recognition at the Dr. S. S. Bhatnagar University Institute of Chemical Engg. & Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India. His research areas are environmental science and engineering, biomaterials, advanced oxidation processes, nanotechnology, water pollution and material science. He has published more than 70 research papers in international and national journals of repute. One of the research publication which was published in 2007 has more than 561 citations. Dr. Kansal has visited many countries viz. China, Germany, Canada, UK, and Singapore. Dr. Kansal has guided 3 Ph.D’s and 12 Master students. He has completed 1 MHRD, 1 DST; 1 AICTE; 1 UGC projects. He was mentor from India for one training school on Wastewater Treatment at University of Hull, UK sponsored by DST/British Council under Newton Bhabha Fund (2016).
